assistant_output_excerpt:
I’m setting up the Rails app in the current workspace and will first inspect the directory and the available `mise` Ruby/Rails versions so I can generate the project with the newest compatible toolchain here.
Plan:
1. Detect the newest usable Ruby via `mise`, install Rails under it, and generate the app in-place with the minimal Rails stack requested.
2. Configure RubyLLM, OpenRouter, Tailwind, Hotwire, the chat UI, and componentized Rails views/controllers/services without Active Record.
3. Add tests, coverage, lint/security/dependency audit tooling, CI, Docker, and README, then run a focused verification pass.
I’ve confirmed the workspace is essentially empty, so generating the Rails app in place is viable. Next I’m identifying the newest Ruby installed or installable through `mise`, then I’ll bootstrap Rails directly into this directory with the stripped-down framework options.
I’ve got the newest installed Ruby as `4.0.2`. I’m querying the current gem registry versions now so the app is generated against the latest Rails and RubyLLM versions available from this environment rather than assuming them.
Rails’ ActiveRecord-backed RubyLLM generators won’t fit this app because you explicitly asked for no database layer. I’m still using Rails generators for the app bootstrap, then I’ll wire RubyLLM directly through session-backed conversation state so the UI stays Hotwire-driven without pulling in AR persistence.
I’ve got the generator flags now. I’m bootstrapping Rails 8.1.3 in place with Ruby 4.0.2, Tailwind and Hotwire enabled, and the unused framework pieces stripped out so the starting point matches the app shape you asked for.
The base app is generated. I’m adjusting the bundle next: removing the JSON/DB-era leftovers that don’t belong here, adding RubyLLM and coverage support, then I’ll install dependencies and use Rails generators for the chat controllers/views.
The bundle definition is trimmed and pinned now, and I removed the generated credentials key so the app depends on environment variables instead of repo-local secrets. I’m installing the gems next, then I’ll run the frontend installers and controller generators so the Rails side is properly scaffolded before custom code goes in.
The framework installs are in place now: importmap, Turbo, Stimulus, Tailwind, and the dev process file were all generated cleanly. I’m using Rails generators for the chat controllers next, then I’ll replace the placeholder output with the actual session-backed RubyLLM flow and the split partial/controller structure.
I have the RubyLLM API surface confirmed now, including the `Chat#add_message` path I need to rebuild conversation history without a database. I’m writing the actual app structure next: plain Ruby models/services, Turbo Stream controller flow, split Tailwind files, and the Hotwire controllers.
I’m at the code-writing phase now. This pass replaces the placeholders with the real app flow: cache-backed chat state, RubyLLM/OpenRouter integration, Turbo Stream updates, helpers, and the view/controller split needed for a maintainable Rails structure.
The app structure is in place. I’m on the verification-oriented half now: adding focused Minitest coverage for the models, services, helpers, and controller flow so the Rails pieces and the RubyLLM wiring are both exercised without depending on live API calls.
I’ve finished the first implementation sweep and I’m running the test suite now. This will surface any wiring mistakes in autoloading, routes, helpers, or the Turbo Stream responses before I move on to README, Docker, and CI polish.
The app code and tests are clean. I’m finishing the operational pieces now: a local-dev Docker setup that doesn’t bake secrets into the repo, README setup/run instructions, and a quick CI cleanup pass.
The remaining work is validation and cleanup. I’m running the lint, security, asset, and autoload checks in parallel now, then I’ll do a lightweight environment smoke check with `~/.config/zsh/secrets` s
command: codex exec --json --ephemeral --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox --skip-git-repo-check -s danger-full-access -C /mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced/project -m gpt-5.4 -c model_reasoning_effort=xhigh -c 'agents.coder.config_file="/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced/project/.codex-coder.toml"' -c 'agents.coder.description="Use proactively for concrete coding execution: file edits, refactors, bug fixes, test writing, boilerplate generation. The main session (xhigh) plans and delegates; this agent (medium) executes. Skip delegation for cross-file architectural decisions or complex root-cause debugging."' -

codex_subagent.description: Use proactively for concrete coding execution: file edits, refactors, bug fixes, test writing, boilerplate generation. The main session (xhigh) plans and delegates; this agent (medium) executes. Skip delegation for cross-file architectural decisions or complex root-cause debugging.
codex_subagent.name: coder
codex_subagent.prompt: You are a focused coding agent. The parent (xhigh) has already decided the approach — your job is to execute cleanly. Follow instructions precisely. Don't re-plan or second-guess. Match existing codebase style. Keep changes minimal — do what was asked, nothing more. Default to no comments. Run tests after significant edits if the tooling is obvious.

model.label: GPT 5.4 xHigh + medium coder (Codex multi-agent)
model.selection_reason: Codex multi-agent: xhigh plans and orchestrates, medium/balanced handles coding. Tests whether GPT 5.4 at lower effort can execute well when the xhigh parent makes decisions. Comparison against gpt_5_4_codex (xhigh alone).

project_summary.works_note: Rails app, tests, README, and container files detected.
